mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 4.4i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X5 4.4i engine typically costs £2,500–£9,000 supply only depending on generation. M62-era engines sit at the lower end (£2,500–£6,500), while N62 units run £3,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £900–£2,000 depending on workshop location. Replacement engines are supplied UK-rebuilt with a 12-month warranty. Main dealer new engines are no longer available. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 4.4i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which of the two engine generations your X5 4.4i has. The M62 (E53 1999–2003) is otherwise bulletproof but timing chain guides are the critical failure point — symptoms typically begin at 80,000–100,000 miles. The N62 (E53 LCI/E70 2003–2010) avoids the guide issue but is notorious for valve stem seal failure, oil leaks, and coolant transfer pipe problems. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 4.4i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain guide failure on the M62 (E53 1999–2003), valve stem seal failure on the N62 (2003–2010) — a £3,000+ repair at around 130,000 miles, oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and timing covers, coolant transfer pipe seal failure on the N62, and general cooling system issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 4.4i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold: if your X5 4.4i is worth £7,000 and a recon engine is £5,000 (71%), it's borderline. If the vehicle is a tired £4,500 example, the same engine exceeds 70% — get a scrap valuation first. The X5 4.4i's V8 appeal and enthusiast following often justify replacement spend if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 4.4i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E53 1999–2003 is M62B44TU (286 bhp), E53 LCI 2003–2006 is N62B44 (315 bhp), E70 2006–2010 is N62B44 (315 bhp). The M62's timing chain guides are the critical failure point; the N62 is notorious for valve stem seal issues. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N62 valve stem seals, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The valve stem seals can be replaced — though it's an engine-out job (12–18 hours) costing £3,000–£5,000. A special tool allows replacement without removing the cylinder heads, but the job remains complex. If caught early, seal replacement restores the engine. If ignored, oil consumption worsens and catalytic converters can be damaged.

rows.eraNote: Timing chain guides fail at 80k–100k miles — an engine-out repair

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: No longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect both M62 and N62 engine generations. See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. Replacement engines are supplied UK-rebuilt with a 12-month warranty and OEM-spec parts.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X5 4.4i engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The V8's tight engine bay adds significant labour time. N62 valve stem seal replacement is a 12–18 hour engine-out job. M62 timing chain guide replacement is also labour-intensive.

cta.label: → Compare X5 4.4i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M62 Timing Chain Guide Failure (E53 1999–2003)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or "slap" from the timing chain, excessive vibration, plastic particles in the oil, check engine light. Symptoms typically begin between 80,000 and 100,000 miles. If left untreated, the chain can jump out of time, causing piston-valve contact.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–100,000 miles — guides must be replaced every 150,000–200,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling or "slap", book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The M62's timing chain guides are its Achilles' heel. The plastic guides become brittle with age and break, sending debris into the oil system. If the chain jumps timing, it can cause catastrophic engine damage. At the first sign of rattling, book an inspection immediately. Preventative replacement is far cheaper than a full engine rebuild. Replace the chain tensioner every 50,000–100,000 miles to extend guide life.

problems.title: N62 Valve Stem Seal Failure (E53 LCI / E70 2003–2010)
problems.symptoms: Blue or white smoke from the exhaust on cold start, high oil consumption (burning 1L per 1,000 miles), rough idle, misfires on cold start, fouled spark plugs. The issue is often worse in cold weather and improves when warm. A $4,000+ repair at around 130,000 miles.
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000–130,000 miles — seals harden with heat and age.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil consumption worsens and catalytic converters can be damaged.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see blue smoke on startup, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The N62's valve stem seals are its most notorious failure. The seals harden over time and allow oil to leak into the combustion chamber. A special tool allows replacement without removing the cylinder heads, but the job remains complex. At the first sign of blue smoke on startup, have the valve stem seals inspected. Some specialists recommend using a valve seal conditioning additive as a temporary measure, but replacement is the only permanent fix.

problems.title: Oil & Coolant Leaks (M62 & N62)
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, visible oil on the engine, low oil level warnings, coolant loss, white smoke from engine bay.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — age-related as gaskets and seals degrade.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,000 (valve cover gaskets, upper timing cover gaskets) / £500–£1,500 (alternator bracket gasket, coolant transfer pipe).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil leaks can lead to oil starvation or belt failure.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The M62 is described as "the leakiest engine BMW ever made". N62 engines are equally notorious for oil leaks from valve cover gaskets, upper timing cover gaskets, and the alternator bracket gasket. Coolant transfer pipe seal failure is also common on the N62. Regular inspection of gaskets and seals is essential. If you notice a burning oil smell or visible oil, have the engine inspected promptly. Address coolant leaks immediately to avoid overheating.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 4.4i? Check These First
buyIf: * E53 M62 with timing chain guides recently replaced — the chain is the critical failure point
* E53 LCI/E70 N62 with valve stem seals replaced — the most expensive N62 job done
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* No oil or coolant leaks — gaskets and seals are a common weak point
avoidIf: * E53 M62 with rattling or "slap" from the engine — timing chain guide failure
* N62 with blue smoke on startup — valve stem seal failure (a £3,000+ job)
* Oil or coolant leaks on any generation — gaskets and seals degrade with age
* Poor service history — these V8s are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X5 4.4i is a glorious V8 SUV but one with well-documented engine vulnerabilities. The M62 (1999–2003) is otherwise bulletproof but timing chain guides are the critical failure point. The N62 (2003–2010) avoids the guide issue but introduces valve stem seal failure, oil leaks, and coolant transfer pipe problems. Both engines suffer from cooling system and gasket issues. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £5,000 recon on a £7,000 X5 4.4i is 71% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully. The N62's valve stem seal job is a 12–18 hour engine-out repair.

cta.label: → Compare X5 4.4i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 4.4i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or "slap" (M62 timing chain guides) / blue smoke (N62 valve stem seals)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M62/N62) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Timing Chain Documentation — Any evidence of chain guide replacement on M62? Essential.
* Valve Stem Seal History — Any evidence of seal replacement on N62? Essential over 100,000 miles.
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers, timing covers, and alternator bracket
* Coolant Check — Check level and condition — coolant transfer pipe failure is common on N62
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
